title: The Three Kings' Sons
summary: The checked-in artifact is a pinned repository version of University of Michigan CME item ThreeKSon, a 1997 keyboarded encoding of F. J. Furnivall's 1895 EETS edition. U-M encodes the complete Middle English prose romance on printed pp. 1–207 while omitting the edition's forewords, p. 208 note, and pp. 209–216 glossary. Furnivall edited the text from Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript of the sole surviving Middle English witness, British Library Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v; no direct Furnivall-to-manuscript collation is asserted without further evidence. Leon Kellner separately collated the English text against an unidentified French copy in Paris for a projected Part II that did not appear. The conceptual French source-work relation is recorded in work metadata because the translator's physical exemplar is unknown. A complete Robarts scan of the 1895 volume and its OCR/scandata derivatives are cached, as is relevant supporting scholarship. The British Library catalog's former whole-manuscript digital link is currently marked unavailable, so no manuscript facsimile is claimed as downloaded; a complete-codex request route is documented instead.
review_notes: The lineage primary subject is the repository artifact, not the abstract romance.
The cataloged work is the complete Middle English romance at Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v; the Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV at ff. 1r–7r is adjacent codex content, not part of ThreeKSon.
Ward identifies forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters marked by illuminated initials. Furnivall's running glosses provide the displayed chapter labels, and U-M reuses those labels as DIV1 heads.
Part I is a bibliographic promise of a projected scholarly continuation, not an incomplete medieval text: the narrative reaches its explicit on printed p. 207.
The Mrs Kellner transcript and Leon Kellner French collation are modeled as distinct editorial intermediaries. No claim is made that Kellner's French collation changed the published Part I.

The physical French object used by Leon Kellner and the physical exemplar used by the medieval translator are both unidentified; neither is equated with BnF français 92 or another surviving French witness.
The current British Library c. 1475–c. 1485 date is preferred for Harley MS 326 while Ward's about-1500 description and Furnivall's title-page wording remain attributed historical assertions.

French belongs to the source-work and Kellner-comparison layers. The work text is Middle English, with a two-line Latin witness colophon; four later-hand English lines are annotation rather than an intrinsic verse section.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for ThreeKSon
entities.description: The exact XML file checked out through the pinned CME submodule in General Corpus.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ThreeKSon
entities.description: A 1997 SGML/XML-style digital text keyboarded by Sencor and reported as 5% proofed, encoding Furnivall pp. 1–207 and omitting the edition's front and back matter. Glenn Wright marked up the text and Jason Chu reviewed the markup.
notes: The current public U-M interface displays all forty-five headings but supplies no separately checksummed source XML download used in this review.
No Oxford Text Archive identifier or transfer statement appears in the header.

entities.label: F. J. Furnivall, The Three Kings' Sons (1895)
alternate_labels: The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text
entities.description: The complete first printed edition of the Middle English romance, with Furnivall's forewords, the narrative on pp. 1–207, a note on p. 208, and Thomas Austin's glossary on pp. 209–216. The projected Part II was to contain Leon Kellner's introduction and language/source study but was never published.

bibliographic.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text, Early English Text Society Extra Series 67 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1895), vii + 216 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Extra Series 67
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.

notes: The title page's unique-manuscript claim is correct only for the surviving Middle English tradition; the French source work has multiple witnesses.
Furnivall's about-1500 witness date is superseded for preferred current description by the British Library's c. 1475–c. 1485 range.

entities.label: Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript of Harley MS 326
entities.description: An unpublished working copy of the English text made for the Early English Text Society. Furnivall calls it an excellent copy and says he took up the edition after Leon Kellner could not do so.
notes: Furnivall does not state Mrs Kellner's personal name, the transcript's repository, or its later survival.

entities.label: Leon Kellner's unpublished English-French collation
entities.description: A documented but unpublished comparison of the English text with what Furnivall calls its French original in Paris, prepared for the projected Part II.
notes: No surviving notes, published Part II, or evidence that the collation altered Part I was found.

entities.label: Unidentified Paris copy of Les trois fils de rois used by Leon Kellner
entities.description: The physical comparison object behind Kellner's collation. Furnivall does not supply a shelfmark, repository, format, or recension; the neutral repository-artifact type avoids falsely choosing one surviving French manuscript.
notes: This object is not identified with BnF français 92, the former No. 6766 discussed by Ward, or any other known French witness.
It is also not asserted to be the medieval English translator's physical exemplar.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 326
entities.description: A composite codex whose paper ff. 1r–7r contain a separate Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV and whose parchment ff. 8r–123v contain the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript of The Three Kings' Sons. The romance has forty-five unnumbered chapters indicated by illuminated initials and twenty-two miniatures.

date_statements.label: Ward's historical catalog date
date_statements.notes: Retained as an attributed older description, not the preferred current date.

holding.notes: All manuscripts and archives must be consulted in London; the catalog says a letter of introduction is required.

physical_description.support: Paper at ff. 1–7; parchment at ff. 8–123
physical_description.extent: 123 folios plus six unfoliated parchment flyleaves: three before the foliation and three after it
physical_description.format: Quarto codex in post-1600 brown leather binding
notes: The target romance occupies parchment ff. 8r–123v; the paper chronicle at ff. 1r–7r is outside the work.
The current catalog gives England, probably London, as witness origin and describes Gothic cursive script.
Four half-page and eighteen smaller miniatures accompany the romance.
Unique means the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, not the only material witness to the French source romance.
The four much-later-hand English lines above the Latin colophon are annotation, not part of the medieval translation.

entities.label: Robarts scan of Furnivall's 1895 Three Kings' Sons
entities.description: A complete Internet Archive delivery of the Robarts Library copy, with a 232-page text PDF, full OCR text, and complete scan metadata ZIP.
entities.reproduction_format: 232-page text PDF with OCR text and Scribe scandata ZIP derivatives
notes: Internet Archive's creator field includes David Aubert, but current scholarship does not support adopting Aubert as author of the French romance.

entities.label: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record for Harley MS 326
entities.description: The current institutional record for the full codex, its two content blocks, dates, materials, origin, decoration, access conditions, and unavailable legacy digitized object.

entities.label: ARLIMA record for The Three Kings' Sons
entities.description: A current repertory record identifying the English prose romance as a translation of Les trois fils de rois, listing Harley MS 326 as its manuscript and Furnivall 1895 as its edition.

entities.label: Ruth Helen Caddick, Clariodus study, volume 1 (2023)
entities.description: A doctoral thesis used as supporting scholarship for the date, dialect, unique English witness, French source, anonymity, and Aubert-attribution caution. It is not upstream of Furnivall or U-M.

bibliographic.citation: Ruth Helen Caddick, A New Edition and Study of the Older Scots Romance 'Clariodus' (PhD thesis, University of Birmingham, 2023), vol. 1, pp. 61–62.
bibliographic.title: A New Edition and Study of the Older Scots Romance 'Clariodus'

entities.label: British Library digitisation and reproduction services price list
entities.description: The complete two-page current rate card linked from the British Library digitisation service. It supplies general rates, not a shelfmark-specific quote for Harley MS 326.

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright
entities.description: The official duration guidance used to assess the United States status of the 1895 edition separately from provider terms and modern files.

agents.name: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
alternate_names: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections

agents.name: Sencor
notes: Named in the XML as the keyboarding agent; no fuller identity is supplied.

agents.name: Mrs Leon Kellner
notes: Furnivall does not give her personal name.

agents.name: ARLIMA — Archives de littérature du Moyen Âge

scope.notes: The shared identifier, header, title, source, contents, and current display establish identity; version_of avoids claiming an undocumented single-copy operation from a currently downloadable U-M XML file.

subject.description: complete encoded Middle English narrative with U-M-supplied structural and illumination annotations

scope.notes: The edition forewords, p. 208 note, Thomas Austin glossary pp. 209–216, and other front/back matter are omitted.
Furnivall explicitly says Mrs Kellner made the copy and that he took up editing the text; his foreword does not separately document a full direct manuscript collation by Furnivall.

subject.description: working transcript of the English romance

assertion.notes: The title page identifies Harley 326 as the unique source manuscript, and the foreword identifies Mrs Kellner's copy as the working text.

object.description: English working text available to Leon Kellner

scope.notes: Furnivall says Kellner collated the English text; the precise physical English copy used for the comparison is not expressly named, so this edge is tentative rather than confirmed.

object.description: unidentified French comparison copy in Paris

scope.notes: The relation records the act Furnivall reports without supplying a shelfmark or identifying the object as the translator's exemplar.

object.description: complete physical codex, with work locus mapped separately
sole listed manuscript witness of the English work

subject.description: comparative discussion of The Three Kings' Sons

notes: The current page offers public reading and searching; no separately checksummed U-M source XML was used.

local_copies.label: Complete Furnivall 1895 text PDF

work_portion.label: Complete Middle English romance
notes: The complete cached source also retains the title pages, forewords, p. 208 note, and pp. 209–216 glossary.
qpdf reported no syntax or stream-encoding errors; all 232 PDF pages rendered successfully at validation resolution.

local_copies.label: Complete Internet Archive OCR text

work_portion.label: OCR for the complete Middle English romance

notes: Valid UTF-8 text; OCR is supporting access and not substituted for the inspected PDF or repository XML.

local_copies.label: Complete Internet Archive Scribe scandata ZIP
notes: The ZIP passed full CRC testing and contains only scan-structure metadata, profiles, PPM data, logs, and backup material; it contains no target-work text or image payload.

access.format: PDF, UTF-8 OCR text, and ZIP scan metadata
notes: Internet Archive supplies no item-specific license or rights statement for this scan.

access.access_method: Open the current Archives and Manuscripts record
The current catalog exposes a legacy full-display link but explicitly labels the digital images currently unavailable
notes: This dated negative finding is not evidence that the manuscript was never digitized.
The catalog also says selected coverage existed in the former Catalogue of Illuminated Manuscripts, but no current exact image payload could be audited.

access.access_method: Register or renew a Reader Pass, submit the online item request with shelfmark Harley MS 326, and consult manuscripts in London; the catalog says a letter of introduction is required
Submit a digitisation order for the complete physical codex and map The Three Kings' Sons ff. 8r–123v separately
access.contact: British Library Digitisation Services; permissions@bl.uk for copying or reproduction permission
access.format: Quote-dependent reference JPEG or reproduction-quality photography
notes: Request the upper and lower boards, spine, pastedowns, gilt edges if offered, all three initial unfoliated flyleaves [i–iii], every surface of fols. 1–123 including the adjacent chronicle, all three terminal unfoliated flyleaves [124–126], and every blank or detached element.
Identify the work portion as ff. 8r–123v without cropping the requested whole codex.
Ask for color photography; BL says original manuscripts cannot be scanned and surrogate scans may be black and white unless color is requested.
Obtain written reuse terms before any public repository or print-on-demand redistribution.

notes: The cached thesis contains locatable supporting discussion of the work at thesis pp. 61–62 (PDF pp. 70–71), but it does not reproduce the target work itself.

qpdf found recoverable linearization hint-table and shared-object inconsistencies but no fatal parse failure; all 291 pages rendered and full text extraction succeeded. Exact downloaded bytes are preserved without repair.

rights.component: Furnivall's 1895 transcription, forewords, editorial glosses and notes, and Austin's glossary
rights.basis: The volume was published in 1895. U-M currently labels the represented materials public domain and No Copyright–United States, while U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A states that works published before 1 January 1931 are in the public domain in the United States.
notes: This United States determination does not by itself resolve provider terms or every other jurisdiction.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF, OCR, and scandata derivatives of the Robarts copy
rights.basis: The underlying 1895 edition is public domain in the United States and the files are publicly downloadable, but the exact Internet Archive item supplies no license URL or rights statement for its digital derivatives.
notes: Public download is an access fact, not an item-specific open-license grant.

rights.component: Materials presented in the current U-M ThreeKSon item
rights.basis: The current item page expressly says the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States rights statement.
notes: The contract-terms field remains unknown because the embedded 1997 XML notice imposes a redistribution restriction that the current page does not expressly reconcile.

rights.component: Electronic text under the availability statement embedded in the 1997 XML
rights.basis: The embedded notice permits personal use, research, teaching, and linking if the statement is retained, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers; it does not state a copyright status or open license.
notes: This historical provider statement is preserved separately from the current No Copyright–United States claim.

rights.component: Pinned CME repository file and repository-level Unlicense wording
rights.basis: CME/LICENSE releases 'software' under the Unlicense, but the file does not expressly define whether corpus text, third-party edition content, U-M markup, or provider restrictions are included in that software scope.
notes: Do not use the repository license to erase the distinct rights and provider assertions attached to the medieval text, Furnivall edition, U-M encoding, or images.

rights.component: Medieval Middle English textual content at ff. 8r–123v, excluding modern photography and catalog data
rights.basis: The text is attested in a manuscript dated c. 1475–c. 1485 and was published in 1895; the determination does not apply to modern photographs, metadata, or contractual access conditions.

rights.component: Any British Library photographs or images supplied through Imaging Services
rights.basis: No current exact image object was available to inspect. British Library Imaging Services says users must clear relevant third-party rights and must not copy or reproduce supplied material without written British Library permission, which may entail additional fees.
notes: The medieval text's public-domain status does not establish permission to redistribute future supplied image files.

rights.component: Caddick's thesis text, argument, formatting, and incorporated material
rights.basis: The University of Birmingham repository labels both accepted-version thesis volumes Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0.
notes: The local cache is for research inspection; the license does not permit commercial reuse or distribution of derivatives.

rights.component: ARLIMA English-work page text and presentation
rights.basis: The exact ARLIMA page links a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The Three Kings' Sons at Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v, inside a codex also containing a paper chronicle at ff. 1r–7r
editorial_practices.representation: Complete romance on printed pp. 1–207, followed by editorial note and glossary
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall prints the complete romance and excludes the adjacent Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV from the edited work.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters indicated by illuminated initials
editorial_practices.representation: Modern running glosses summarize the narrative at page heads
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall's navigational glosses should not be mistaken for manuscript rubric wording even though their chapter breaks correspond to manuscript structure.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete 1895 volume with forewords, narrative, note, and glossary
editorial_practices.description: U-M explicitly omits the printed edition's front and back matter while retaining the complete medieval narrative.
notes: The omitted matter includes Furnivall's forewords, the p. 208 note, and Thomas Austin's glossary pp. 209–216.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Furnivall's running page glosses over forty-five manuscript-supported chapter boundaries
editorial_practices.representation: Forty-five DIV1 units with those glosses used as HEAD values
editorial_practices.description: U-M turns edition running glosses into structural heads. The first head is explicitly tagged supplied, and the header declaration applies the practice to the DIV1 level generally.

editorial_practices.representation: Bracketed NOTE1 references supplied from the manuscript when not noted in Furnivall's text
editorial_practices.description: U-M's declaration distinguishes newly supplied manuscript-illumination references from Furnivall's printed apparatus.
editorial_practices.scope: Twenty-two illumination-note entries within the complete encoded narrative
notes: The XML contains twenty-four NOTE1 elements in total: twenty-two illumination references, Furnivall's p. 208 peacock note, and the later-hand colophon annotation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: English text and an unidentified French comparison copy in Paris
editorial_practices.representation: Unpublished collation intended for a projected Part II
editorial_practices.description: Leon Kellner compared the English text with a French copy, but Furnivall does not identify the shelfmark or say that the comparison changed Part I.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete English text, exact French comparison scope unknown

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ThreeKSon.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY nodes ThreeKSon:1–45; incipit, explicit, colophon, NOTE1, PB, and MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the exact repository artifact and U-M item identity, 1997 digital publication, Furnivall source edition, Sencor keyboarding and 5% proofing claim, Glenn Wright markup, Jason Chu review, front/back omission, running-gloss headings, supplied illumination references, complete narrative, and embedded provider terms.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML, checksum, Git-object, sequence, and element-count inspection of ThreeKSon.xml.
evidence.locator: Complete file and BODY ancestry-sensitive counts
evidence.summary: Verified 566,842 bytes; SHA-256 9912c653…; Git blob d6a7994f…; 45 sequential DIV1 nodes; 115 work-text P elements outside notes; 207 TEXT-wide PB markers numbered 1–207; 231 leaf milestones from 8v through 123v after the opening f. 8r note; 24 NOTE1; two Q1; and six L elements, of which two Latin colophon lines are outside notes and four later-hand English lines are inside an editorial note.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Contains Unlicense language framed expressly as a release of software; it does not define the scope of corpus texts, third-party editions, provider terms, or manuscript images.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The Three Kings' Sons, item ThreeKSon.
evidence.locator: Item information, rights statement, and forty-five-entry contents list
evidence.summary: Confirms the public digital item, stable UMDL link, Furnivall editor and 1895 publication, all forty-five displayed headings, and current Public Domain / No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text, EETS Extra Series 67 (London, 1895), complete Robarts scan.
evidence.locator: Title page; forewords pp. v–vii; complete text pp. 1–207; p. 208 note; glossary pp. 209–216
evidence.summary: Direct inspection establishes Furnivall's title, editor credit, EETS series and imprint, complete narrative and explicit, the unique Harley source claim, Mrs Leon Kellner's working copy, Leon Kellner's Paris French collation, the projected Part II, running glosses and manuscript locators, and the extent of editorial back matter omitted by U-M.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for threekingssonsen00aubeuoft, contributed by Robarts Library, University of Toronto.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1895 Robarts source copy and the exact PDF, OCR, and scandata derivatives and byte sizes. The metadata supplies no licenseurl, rights, or usage field and incorrectly includes David Aubert in the creator field.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 326, record 040-002046154.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content; Decoration; date, origin, extent, physical description, access, digitized-content status; Ward 1883 excerpt
evidence.summary: The current institutional record maps the Chronicle to ff. 1r–7r and The Three Kings' Sons to ff. 8r–123v, gives the romance incipit, explicit, and Latin colophon, dates the codex c. 1475–c. 1485, locates witness production in England probably London, describes paper/parchment support and six flyleaves, inventories twenty-two miniatures, cites the unique manuscript edition, and marks the legacy digital images currently unavailable. Its Ward excerpt describes forty-five unnumbered chapters indicated by illuminated initials and preserves the older about-1500 date.

evidence.citation: British Library legacy full-display route for Harley MS 326 and current Archives and Manuscripts availability state.
evidence.summary: On 15 July 2026 the current exact catalog record expressly labeled the former full digital object unavailable, the legacy full-display route did not yield an auditable item viewer, and the cited Catalogue of Illuminated Manuscripts route did not expose a current exact complete image payload. No lawful public complete-codex facsimile was therefore cached.
notes: This is a dated negative access result, not proof that the codex was never digitized or that selected legacy images never existed.

evidence.locator: Online ordering, required shelfmark/folio/format information, manuscript photography, color requests, and reuse warning
evidence.summary: Provides the current online order route and says orders need shelfmark, title, and recto/verso folio details; original manuscripts require photography rather than scanning; existing surrogate scans may be black and white unless color is requested; and reuse permissions must be checked separately.

evidence.citation: British Library, Imaging Services Terms and Conditions, updated January 2026.
evidence.locator: Preservation of originals; copyright and reproductions
evidence.summary: States that supplied images are delivered page by page, item condition and large orders may require individual treatment, users must investigate third-party rights, and supplied material must not be copied or reproduced without written British Library permission, potentially subject to additional fees.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation and Reproduction Services Price List, 2025/26 rates.
evidence.summary: Current linked general rates for reference JPEG scans, reproduction TIFF photography, bespoke handling, OCR, microfilm, and advanced imaging; not a quote for Harley MS 326. The 181,286-byte complete PDF is cached.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, The Three Kings' Sons, record 2070, updated 24 June 2025.
evidence.summary: Identifies the English work as a prose romance and translation of Les trois fils de rois, lists Harley MS 326 as its manuscript, gives Furnivall's 1895 edition as vii + 216 pages, and supplies ARLIMA and Wikidata identifiers. The page applies CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, Les trois fils de rois, record 331.
evidence.locator: Bibliography, contents, manuscript list, and identifiers
evidence.summary: Describes the anonymous mid-fifteenth-century French prose romance, gives title variants and a detailed plot, says the David Aubert attribution appears false although Aubert copied one manuscript, lists eight surviving French manuscripts and the English translation, and supplies ARLIMA 331, Wikidata Q122389319, and JONAS 4136 identifiers.

evidence.citation: University of Birmingham eTheses, Ruth Helen Caddick, A New Edition and Study of the Older Scots Romance 'Clariodus' (2023), record 13262.
evidence.summary: Confirms Caddick's authorship, 2023 PhD status, accepted-version volume downloads, and Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 license.

evidence.citation: Ruth Helen Caddick, A New Edition and Study of the Older Scots Romance 'Clariodus' (PhD thesis, University of Birmingham, 2023), vol. 1, pp. 61–62.
evidence.summary: Describes The Three Kings' Sons as a late-fifteenth-century English prose romance surviving uniquely in Harley MS 326, gives the British Library's c. 1475–c. 1485 range and a southern English dialect, identifies the mid-fifteenth-century French prose source and its alternate titles, states that both French author and English translator are unknown, and reports Grinberg's rejection of a likely Aubert authorship.
notes: The complete 3,305,865-byte volume is cached under its CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license. Recoverable PDF linearization warnings were retained without rewriting the downloaded bytes.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.locator: PDF p. 2, works already under statutory protection before 1978
evidence.summary: Official guidance says the maximum total term for pre-1978 protected works is ninety-five years and that works published in the United States before 1 January 1931 are in the public domain. The complete 273,867-byte circular is cached.

evidence.citation: Integrity, rendering, archive, checksum, byte-count, and hard-link audit of source-cache/ThreeKSon.
evidence.locator: All six cached files and canonical hard-link counterparts
evidence.summary: Verified six files in both the research worktree and canonical General Corpus cache with matching inodes, byte sizes, SHA-256 values, and byte-for-byte comparisons. The Furnivall PDF passed qpdf cleanly and all 232 pages rendered; the Caddick thesis, BL rate card, and Copyright Office circular had recoverable linearization-hint warnings but all 291, 2, and 4 pages rendered; the OCR is valid UTF-8; and the scandata ZIP passed complete CRC testing.

open_questions.question: Which French recension and physical copy was the medieval translator's exemplar, and which Paris copy did Leon Kellner collate?
next_steps: Inspect Henry Grinberg's complete 1975 manuscript-filiation article and Giovanni Palumbo's 2001 edition and 2002 manuscript study.
Search EETS archival correspondence and Kellner papers for a Paris shelfmark or collation notebook.
Keep the abstract translation relation in work metadata and do not identify BnF français 92 or another extant French witness as the physical exemplar without provenance evidence.

open_questions.question: Do Mrs Leon Kellner's transcript and Leon Kellner's French collation survive, and did Furnivall directly recheck the full text against Harley MS 326?
next_steps: Search Early English Text Society archives, Furnivall papers, and Kellner papers for the transcript, proofs, and correspondence.
Do not add a direct Furnivall-to-Harley transcription or collation edge until an edition statement, proof record, or correspondence documents that consultation.
If the working materials survive, compare them with the printed edition to identify Furnivall's interventions.

open_questions.question: Can the British Library restore or supply a complete current color facsimile of Harley MS 326 with explicit reuse terms?
next_steps: Ask whether the unavailable legacy full-display object or selected illuminated-manuscript images can be restored through a current exact delivery endpoint.
If not, order the complete codex: binding, pastedowns, edges if offered, all six unfoliated flyleaves, every surface of ff. 1–123, blanks, and detached matter; map the romance ff. 8r–123v separately.
Request color photography and written permission terms for repository and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand reuse before publishing images.
After receipt, verify every chapter initial, miniature, folio boundary, and later annotation against the edition and XML.
notes: The general rate card is not a shelfmark-specific quote.

open_questions.question: Does U-M's current Public Domain / No Copyright–United States statement supersede the embedded 1997 prohibition on redistribution by commercial and not-for-profit content providers?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for a written interpretation covering raw XML, close derivatives, repository redistribution, and print-on-demand outputs.
Until clarified, preserve both dated assertions and distinguish copyright status from provider terms.
Do not infer that the CME repository's software-framed Unlicense automatically resolves the older U-M restriction.

open_questions.question: Was an Oxford Text Archive object or another electronic intermediary used before the 1997 U-M encoding?
next_steps: Search historical OTA catalog exports and U-M project documentation by title, UMDL identifier, edition, and source checksum.
Compare any recovered OTA file structurally and bytewise before adding an entity or directional edge.
Do not infer an OTA layer from patterns elsewhere in the corpus: this header names U-M HTI, Sencor, Glenn Wright, and Jason Chu but no OTA identifier.
